data.epi_content:
I had just obliterated the girl I was trying to save with my own hands, leaving no trace behind.

The girl named Erica shouldn't be remembered that way.

The black magician recalls the girl he had just held lightly in his arms.

Without limbs, she was much lighter than most children's wooden dolls.

Where there had once been eyes—dull, yet capable of conveying emotion—nothing remained except traces of what had been dried-up eyes.

The hands that occasionally pointed at him, poured delicious tea, and served him tea were gone.

The thin arms that seemed barely able to hold anything had left no trace, completely crushed and burned away, with no evidence they had ever existed.

The neat feet that sometimes showed when she wore open-toed shoes were no longer there.

The thighs, though thin, had once shown enough contour to identify her as a woman when wearing pants, but they were nowhere to be seen.

Her clothes were just multiple layers of tattered rags.

Where her eyes had once been, there were twisted, dried brown bloodstains, as if she had shed tears of blood.

Was this truly the same girl the cursed magician had vowed to save?

If he were to revive this corpse that deserved immediate rest, wouldn't it be merely for his own satisfaction?

Shouldn't he send her back to the past right away?

He couldn't use the girl's body as a sacrifice to evil gods.

To the black magician who had lived through countless lifetimes—not just one—trying to save this girl who had curled up against the world's accusations and threats until only her head and torso remained, Erica was something sacred.

And then he remembered.

Perhaps, if he succeeded in destroying the Empire this time, he could return to that time with Erica.

His thoughts raced quickly.

It was meaningless to burden Erica with any more pain.

Evan had made a promise to Erica.

That he would save her, that he would protect her.

When Erica heard those words, others might say her expression didn't change at all, but she slightly raised the corners of her mouth and then let them drop. She looked like she wanted to cry, but either her tears had dried up or she couldn't shed tears because she had no eyes.

The Crown Prince made her shoot herself in the neck, and as she was shot and dragged away, I could only stand and watch.

For the price of a few million, that was all I could do.

In exchange for witnessing Erica's loss before my eyes, I was helped to return to the past.

While hearing the mocking voice of the evil god saying it was better than remembering after death, I was firmly restrained from rushing out, my arms bruised from being constrained as I tried to cast spells, forced to watch without even closing my eyes.

The sight of Erica futilely clutching at the dirt, leaving red blood on the ground as she was dragged away.

After the girl was taken, I thought this would happen.

I wanted to storm the Imperial Palace immediately, but I thought rather than wasting this life and opportunity in vain, I should save her with certainty—but was that just my own delusion?

Let me put aside these negative thoughts creeping up.
data.epi_content2:
It's enough to recall such things when seeing the girl I want to save dying, slowly withering away, living a fate worse than death.

Isn't this situation familiar?

Familiar, even if the pain in my heart never becomes any easier to bear.

One of the largest countries on the continent should be enough to go back without restrictions.

Taking with me old memories, practiced know-how, and ancient emotions.

So the magician begins to steady his breathing, so that the next time he meets the girl, instead of confronting confused emotions as usual, he can smile and tell her he will live for her.

He blows off the head of the person holding the pole where the woman is hanging, then summons wind to make her fall somewhere with less impact.

They probably brought the girl as some kind of trap.

But traps mean nothing. Since the day I saw Erica being dragged away, I've always worked alone.

Normally, a battle that would end in half a day dragged on for more than a day.

They certainly brought numerous troops, but did they still not realize that those poor soldiers clutching only spears or guns would merely become nourishment?

In the end, as always, the black magician won.

After all, if you cut through the pawns and behead the general, it's over.

There were hardly any living people, let alone corpses maintaining their proper form.

And splashing through puddles of blood, he approaches the girl he wanted to save, but who was already broken.

And he called her name.

He held the girl he kept alive in this moment because he still had words to convey, pressed their foreheads together, and whispered to her.

That he knew she would return when she died.

That next time he would find her.

That this would never happen again.

That he would protect her.

Then he obliterated the girl.

Leaving no trace that her body had ever existed.

As if a woman named Erica had never remained in such an ugly state.

And clutching the edge of the rags that had scattered from his hands, he murmured softly.

Even if I have to burn everything in the world as a sacrifice.

The black magician sets off again to find a new sacrifice.

His steps are directed toward the capital of the Empire.

Afterward, the legendary black magician who suddenly appeared and spent nearly three years burning the entire continent, becoming synonymous with disaster, sacrificed the Emperor and countless others, seemingly becoming the new order replacing the Empire.

Whether this new order was positive or negative didn't really matter.

For the people of the continent living through daily hell, they just wanted one side to win quickly and end this hell.

Some were even glad that, despite a few being sacrificed, this catastrophe might finally end.

For now, it was still hope rather than certainty.
data.epi_content3:
Half the continent's population—not the Empire's—was gone.

It wasn't exactly half, but regardless of social status, children who hadn't lost one parent—mostly fathers who had been conscripted—were rare, and many were starving because evil energy had been scattered on the land, making it impossible to grow food.

In other words, for those with power and the ability to conduct themselves properly, an opportunity had arrived.

The population had decreased, but the food in the granaries remained, and with the Emperor—perhaps the order of the Empire itself—gone, they could do as they pleased.

In such times, renowned figures begin to emerge.

Someone who kills one or two people is a murderer, but someone who slaughters hundreds on the battlefield is called a hero.

Though countless people were sacrificed, the nobles' and privileged classes' atrocities, plundering, and deaths in battle after conscription were also numerous, so ordinary people stopped caring who won.

Whether dying of starvation or being sacrificed, it was the same miserable death.

Some even thought being sacrificed might be better, as it meant dying in an instant.

Violence was just violence.

Later, the victor might proclaim their righteousness while exposing the loser's evil, but now there was no winner or loser yet.

And for the people who hoped that one side would win and end this chaotic era, it was devastating when the black magician was killed in the northern Grand Duchy, which had declared independence after what was once the Empire—and the Emperor—was roasted over the course of a day (precisely a day and a half, they say).

The black magician called Vivian had his limbs blown off, and even with his remaining body emitting evil energy and firing malicious spells randomly until the end, he was killed when a knight blessed by a cleric called "the betrayer of his kind" pierced his heart.

They say that even as he was being butchered at the end, he laughed bizarrely, mocking them.

Muttering unintelligible words about how the time had finally come to reap the fruits of his efforts.

...............

Trembling hands, hot tea spilled on my leg.

It's fine. My legs are still attached, and I felt the pain.

Stimulation is truly important.

It's the only thing that helps me live as myself.

Whether it's pleasure or pain doesn't matter.

"Evan, Evan saved me."

I had been tormenting myself, thinking it would be a hopeless wish, that Evan couldn't possibly save me, that he couldn't possibly care about me that much.

I knew very well that intense emotions felt in a moment become diluted with time, just as they do for me.

"But how on earth..."

No matter how many times time repeats, I always stand in the same place, while Evan seems to be gradually approaching me.

"Haha, I don't know. I really don't know."

During my time imprisoned in the Imperial Palace, I was desperate for tobacco.

Right now, Johannes, that madman, should be wandering around the shop, so I decided to wait a while before going.

I'll just wait for Evan while drinking fragrant tea.

He told me to wait, after all.
